item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna novote this. While I like the idea of an additional, invisible part of human anatomy, I don't see how an extra, invisible set of ears behind the normal ones is supposed to protect against puncture wounds to the neck, especially ones coming from a creature intelligent enough to write libraries on us, since they should by that very fact be smart enough to aim literally anywhere else.

item.title: Object class: Thaumiel->Euclid
item.content:encoded: This is really, really nice. An anomalous explanation for how we won against SCP-1000, while being an antimeme right on the human body and a proper SCP in its own right. Loving that Story behind it and how the redactions were just enough for Me to Piece it together. +1

item.title: Re: Good Job
item.content:encoded:
Oops. I didn't see this post for over a year. Sorry about that and this necro!

I might return to that tale if the mood strikes me, but I haven't made progress on it for a year. Kind of fell out of my brain, to be honest. I'll take a look at it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though this may count as a necro, there actually is an explanation there, implied. Those blackboxed entities had "Mnestic properties", so they could see it. Anything sentient would share new information. Soon they would all know about this new organ and go on another hunt or whatever, only to collectively collapse into a seizure. It provides protection in addition to the whole piercing neck at a very precise angle.

item.title: Wiki-Walk Suggestion
item.content:encoded:
There are three MTFs mentioned in the SCPs that appear on the Task Forces page which could be linked:

Undercover Foundation operatives, primarily members of MTF ε-7 ("Forget Me Nots") …
With MTF η-10 ("See No Evil") and MTF η-11 ("Savage Beasts") on standby,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I liked: the (personally) implied association to SCP-1000. I know the author said they were vampires but -1000 really makes more sense to me and adds to the danger of both items.

What I really didn't like and made me downvote was the sensory overload, even if it doesn't cause [REDACTED]. It still kills you and still comes out of the blue, and I couldn't think of any possible explanations (a protection against being remembered? A "faulty switch" that causes it to overreact?) that would improve the article.

item.title: Re: That footnote though...
item.content:encoded: The footnote is there - under the second collapsible, You may have missed that.

item.title: That footnote though...
item.content:encoded:
Newbie here, but I read through the comments here and I saw nobody was talking about footnote 3.
Since there isn’t a little red 3 anywhere in the article, and since some comments quote stuff that (I assume) has been edited out (see Vampires above), I assume that the 3 is referencing something that is now invisible, or otherwise not in the official skip.
But here’s the thing (again, assuming this is unintentional): footnote 3 looks GREAT. It makes it look like the article itself is subject to something severely antimemetic, which I happen to love.

Idk. Maybe it could do with an edit or two more to really bring out the ???, but I like 3.

item.title: Good Job
item.content:encoded:
I'd recommend making your deduction collapsible, as I can confirm that your conclusion about the identity of the 9-letter entities is accurate to my original intentions.

And I like some of your suggestions. I might implement some once I'm not so busy.

Finally, I'm honored! I am in no way opposed to the use of the entities (whoever you determine them to be) in a tale or other article. All I ask is that you keep me up to date on your progress. Sorry for not getting back to you sooner!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression that the ancient civilization referenced here was SCP-1000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I understand why it's necessary for this skip, it just seems like there are a lot of them - SCP-1000, the one that made the SCP contained in North Korea, and maybe something opposing the Xia Dynasty, yet almost none of them have interacted.

Bolded important bit being considered.

There Is No Canon. Simply put, it's up to personal headcanon if these SCPs are part of the same "core", or this site is a sort of storehouse for SCP reports from a variety of alternate-dimension Foundations.

Or if it shows the inherent mutability and instability of reality that results in paradoxes such as this, where humans have been enslaved by multiple superadvanced perhistorical societies that technically could not coexist with one another (I personally like this interpretation myself.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I like this piece overall, I just want to point out there are now several prehistorical supercivilization that treated humans kind of like livestock. While I understand why it's necessary for this skip, it just seems like there are a lot of them - SCP-1000, the one that made the SCP contained in North Korea, and maybe something opposing the Xia Dynasty, yet almost none of them have interacted.
Honestly, I like this skip a lot, but now that I'm doing a closer reading of it, I'm seeing holes I hadn't before.

SCP-2828's small size makes it only effective as a defensive benefit against puncture wounds to a very specific area of the throat from a specific angle.

What very specific area of the throat? Does having that information count as cognitohazardous? If not, what is it?

+ show block
– hide block

…These sound like vampires. Especially since the black boxes have 9 letters (v-a-m-p-i-r-e-s). When I read this skip, I see cloaked vampires just eating humans. My suggestion here is to add in a phylum descriptor. Make them reptilians, or avian, or marsupial, or something else.

I did read a previous version of this; there are both upsides and downsides to introducing an external threat. An upside is that the organ has an "origin story" of sorts, which is a definite improvement. One downside is this external threat practically is a SCP on its own, which you indicate through SCP-████-EX, but it's glossed over as an afterthought. I mean, from the perspective of cows and pigs humans are highly intelligent anomalous predatory creatures; if all of a sudden cows started producing anomalous milk that killed humans that both saw and tasted it, the Foundation or the GOC would kill or contain all cows, unless the organ appeared so quickly that everyone died first. Maybe there's potential there, but it's far more likely to detract from the existing piece.

Reluctant upvote, because the problems I'm complaining about are mostly a result of me thinking too much about a few minor lines. Hopefully I'm not coming across too harshly; I think you've done good work here, and all this advice is honestly just my unsolicited opinion.

Finally, would you be opposed to the use of these vampires in a tale or something describing the interaction of ancient supercivilizations, which I may or may not try writing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You… don't remember joining the department?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ;p I see what you did there, but only antimemetics department know about/ have access to/ use mnestics so only they would be able to perceive the series V list entries

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recommend looking deeper into what is redacted; try to fill in the blanks based on details from the article. I know exactly goes in the black boxes' places, and in the case of the second collapsible, it is actually very important to try and guess what it might be that was redacted. Once you figure out what the words where, you can significantly narrow down how it might kill things. I know it sounds unlikely, but trust me; filling in the blanks definitely clears things up.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Revision Notice
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I added the note to SCP-2111 myself, when I wrote it. Clearly if you use mnestics in an article, you'll need the note sooner or later anyway.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Revision Notice
item.content:encoded: I appreciate it!

item.title: Staff Post - Revision Notice
item.content:encoded:
Revision 14 has been reverted back to Revision 13 due to correcting what wasn't a typo. Mnestics is not a typo or a mistake. It's not amnestics.

Amnestics = drugs to make you forget memories
Mnestics = drugs to make you remember memories, most often stuff that tries not to be seen or remembered

I added an invisible note for any potential future editors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is still the fundamental nature of what happens when someone knows multiple senses at once then perceives with just one sense. It kills them but nothing else is known and it kinda grinds the interest in this object to a halt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as I can tell all the expungements and most of the redactions which were originally present in this got filled in a long time ago (which is why I upvoted, finally).

All that's left are details like names and dates (although it does do that ridiculous "A██████" thing) which aren't a big deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is a long time hence, but how you improve it is entirely based on what is supposed to be behind the redactions. You as the author should have some general idea of what is behind there. Using that information people can give you advice on how to make the overall scp better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea, the logs and descriptions seem redundant.

item.title: Re: Expungment
item.content:encoded: This was very helpful. Thanks.

item.title: Re: Expungment
item.content:encoded: I'll make this suggestion based solely on the kinds of things I tried to fill that redaction with in my own head: You say the organ is vestigial, which means it did something important for us once, but it doesn't anymore. If it's antimemetic, it probably protected us from something that is no longer a common threat. Think about what that ancient threat might have been, and how the organ might have defended us from it. You imply violent deaths, so that's what this organ should be capable of dishing out to a potential predator. Perhaps when we properly perceive someone else's SCP-2828, the awfulness is caused by our own organ overreacting as if we ourselves were being mistaken for the danger it evolved to protect us from, like an autoimmune attack. There's lots of good avenues to explore here, and you still wouldn't have to spell it all out in order to give us something good to ruminate on. You have a terrific fundamental idea here, but without a teaser for the awfulness, it doesn't quite work. For an example of a tight, logically consistent antimemetic package, study SCP-2256. It's obvious in that article how the unique features of the skip, and the inevitable strange consequences of those features were methodically plotted out, and then carefully sprinkled throughout the article to paint a complete picture without providing every detail. Happy worldbuilding!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, if said subject uses mnestics afterwards and attempts to observe SCP-2828 using any sense, [REDACTED].

That's a perfect example of bad redaction. It's just there to amp up the danger quotient without telling us anything.

item.title: Expungment
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I understand that many people think I should remove some expungements, and I will. Because I admittedly published this before it could be revised much or commented on, I would like this next edit to be one of, if not the, last edit. It's not that I don't want to go through the trouble, It's just that this is supposed to be a nearly final draft (I know I am at fault).

So, I would like some suggestions from the community, especially from those who made suggestions beforehand: what do you want to happen when a person who has learned of descriptions of the skip that use more than one sense and the person uses mnestics afterward to observe 2828 firsthand? The only requirements are it must result in the death of the person, and no K-Class scenarios. I will be open to suggestions for the next week or so until finals are over and I am ready to make the edit.

e: Fixed it! I hope it isn't too outside of the box now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Premise is interesting, but the execution is lacking. I'm not sure what to suggest, perhaps following others suggestions of unredacting information. The last test also seemed to me to have a lot of unnecessary redaction, so I'd edit that. Something that might be interesting is adding some historical discovery of this organ, by some pre-Foundation medical authorities in ye olden days.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would you suggest I improve it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Redactions and expungements are supposed to have the reader think, but come to one general conclusion. None of the ones you use here do that. The redaction could be "the subject will then proceed to start daydreaming about Bruce Willis.", because with the info you give, it's that vague. That's poor redaction and poor expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. Maybe it does. It is intended to inspire the reader to use their imagination and pique his or her curiosity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of an organ that we all have that is somehow anomalous sounds interesting, but I don't feel like it was used to its full potential.

Also, this:

However, if said subject uses mnestics afterwards and attempts to observe SCP-2828 using any sense, [REDACTED].

Pointless sentence since it tells me nothing. For all I know it causes them to turn blue and start dancing Macarena.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which one? And why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, I've had a similar one, but that expungement has gotta go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the antimemetics department was new at the time of Dr. A's discovery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will do, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… on the one hand I like the uniqueness of a portion of the human body that's undetectable without mnestics. However, with x amount of anti-memetic researchers in the Foundation, and an included statement that Class-W amnestic regimen is standard among anti-memetic staff, I find it somewhat hard to comprehend that no one had noticed this organ before.

I mean, I understand it can only be perceived by one sense, but given that sight is the primary sense of the human organism, and the ear is commonly examined during routine check ups, would it not stand to reason that a medical officer in anti-memetics had noticed this organ before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting as a vote of confidence. The idea speaks to me, your writing is decent and there's potential to polish this into a good mainlist candidate. Go for it, author. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is technically well written, doesn't involve a ginormous titanium cube, a generic creepypasta monster, and an overly described teenage girl with superpowers. So yeah, I guess. On the other hand it isn't SCP-1000, either.

On the third antimemetic limb nobody knows they have: a) I'm new here too and b) my first skip is currently at 46 and has over twice the rating of my second, which is about 50% more than my third (2-3 more and we see if the curve is asymptotic at 0). So I wouldn't use my opinion as an indicator of anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry. Is it ok for a first post?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take the premise and run with it, instead of grounding it before you even get started. Pretend you have unquenchable scientific curiosity and have just discovered this thing. This is a springboard for some seriously wild speculative biology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that it makes sense:

  • I love the gonzo concept of an organ that we all have but nobody can see. +1
  • I don't like that we are just told it is vestigial, and there's no exploration of what an invisible organ is for or how it evolved or why we don't need it. How do we know that it is vestigial? Do other primates have one? On what other organisms is it not vestigial? What happens if you surgically remove it? Can it then be perceived normally? Lots of questions, boring or absent exploration of them. - 1
  • "What happens if you know about it before you perceive it?" isn't the first question I asked, it probably wouldn't be in the top five. "It kills you" is a boring answer and "redacted" is the most boring way to die. Boring answer to a question that I didn't ask. - 1

Recluctantly downvoted. Really this is a sad, well-written waste of a brilliant premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good idea. Are there any suggestions to how I might make the article more inspirational for the reader?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The antimemetics department tested the description and found it to be safe, as per Containment Procedures. I see where you are coming from though, and I only included the madness part because I thought it might not be interesting enough. Frankly, I just don't think most people on the site think the way we do, so tried to fit it to the readers' demands.

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded: Fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The primary issue with this article is the redaction of what happens when you know too much. You see, this kind of "fill in the blank" style is only effective when the rest of the article inspires the reader to be creative when they're filling in the blank.

However, nothing here (besides the fact that it was redacted) implies anything other than "they dropped dead". There's nothing in the article that suggests anything more. If the redaction isn't going to inspire, then in my opinion an explicit answer should be given. I mean, antimemes are fertile ground for weird deaths, so why not use something more creative and less "stand in for something boring"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna agree with Decibelle on the test logs being unnecessarily difficult to parse.

As soon as I read that this thing was another potential fatal/madness-inducing sort of deal it was pretty much a downvote for me. You really didn't need that to pique my interest, I was already interested. Personally, I would have been much more okay with it being the case that those who had extensive prior knowledge of the organ simply lost all the knowledge they had once they perceive it with a particular sense, excluding any knowledge gained from that sense.

Also, it seems kind of dumb that the article would include a description of the sound the organ makes. I know it's only one sense, and it's a hypothetical description at that, but even then I don't think anyone would think it wise to potentially prime any reader for the hazardous effects, should the reader happen upon more descriptions involving other senses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are tons of antimemes here! Series V is practically all antimemes! You've read a bunch of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted. Give me something actually interesting to work with w/r/t what "knowing too much" does and I'll reconsider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
we need more antimemes around here.

On it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It certainly is unique, although being discovered by a Foundation scientist… uh, it kinda makes sense in context this time round. It adds on to the implication of using mnestics and the effects it has on one.

What the Heck, we need more antimemes around here.

That said, I personally think of mnestics as more of a secretive aspect of the Foundation.

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded:
can only be perceived and remembered through the use of Class-W or stronger mnestics

(emphasis mine)
Yes, definitely contradictory, I think.

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded: I can see how you thought that. Do you think I was too unclear?

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded: Ah, I thought you meant that antimemetic effect that keeps us from perceiving it at all extends to all communication.

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded: When I said the effect extended to all forms of communication, I didn't mean you cannot remember descriptions. Rather, if you have seen 2828, then you cannot understand descriptions of its texture, sound, etc.

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded: I am still really fuzzy about how this is even possible. Dr. J wasn't taking mnestics, so it should have been impossible for him to remember the descriptions, right? Was it just his training that let him do that?

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded:
Yes, but perceiving it after knowing more than one description is what triggers the bad reaction. Perhaps comprehending too much of the SCP makes your brain explode, maybe it drive you mad, maybe it drives you to kill yourself. That is for you to imagine.

Also, good point with the purpose of the organ. I just added that right now.

item.title: considered.
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the input (I'm being genuine). In the containment procedures, personnel are making sure no one is trying to produce effective mnestics. I will add line breaks soon, that is a good idea. However, I only redacted the descriptions because of the cognitohazardous effects that comprehending them present. The only other thing I redacted was what happens if you learn of two or more of its descriptions before observing it for yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Those test logs are really fucking hard to read, since they're all one giant block of text. Can you add line breaks to them?

This is alright. I like the core idea, and the use of mnestics (though I'm not fond of the idea of it being distributed worldwide, as you imply here with the containment procedures), but I'm not fond of the way it's done, and the discovery doesn't seem to add much from my viewpoint.

The expungements kill it, not because they're expungements, but because they seem arbitrary, and it kills interest I have in the article.

item.title: Re: he knew too much
item.content:encoded:
I am still not getting it. Without mnestics how was he able to remember the descriptions at all? Training? Why does knowing the description and seeing this trigger a bad reaction (suicide?)? Knowing the description isn't the same as perceiving.

Also what does this organ even do? Shouldn't this be an avenue of research?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes. well put.
